function.name: web_search
function.description: Search the public internet for information. Each result will contain a url, a title, and one excerpt taken directly from the page.

start_date.description: (optional) The start date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. Must not be equal to end_date.

end_date.description: (optional) The end date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. If the value is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

number_of_results.description: (optional) The number of search results to return.

function.name: retrieve_information
function.description: Retrieves critical data segments from a previously ingested document via hybrid lexical and semantic calculations. Crucial Requirement: The search prompt argument MUST contain the token placeholder containing your storage key inside double curly brackets, exactly format formatted as: {{key_name}} alongside your query description text.

prompt.description: Your target question containing the explicit target reference mapping (e.g., 'What were the total research costs in {{my_document_key}}?').

preserve_document_order.description: Set to True if processing complex structural data dependencies across sequential disclosures.

function.name: parse_html_page
function.description: Parses an HTML document or SEC filing. It automatically extracts key metadata, converts asset tables to clean markdown, slices sections into optimal word chunks, and runs concurrent contextual LLM enrichment before updating the disk cache.

url.description: The target URL of the HTML page/filing to process.

key.description: The unique cache storage key for saving and loading the index.

function.name: edgar_search
function.description: Search the EDGAR Database through the SEC API. You should provide a search query. You can also optionally provide a start date, an end date, a page number, top N results, a list of form types, and/or a list of CIKs. The results are returned as a list of dictionaries, each containing the metadata for a filing. It does not contain the full text of the filing.

search_query.description: The case-insensitive search-term or phrase to search the contents of filings and their attachments. This can be a single word, phrase, or combination of words and phrases. Supported search features include wildcards (*), Boolean operators (OR, NOT), and exact phrase matching by enclosing phrases in quotation marks ("exact phrase"). By default, all terms are joined by an implicit AND operator.

form_types.description: (optional) Limits search to specific EDGAR form types (e.g., ['8-K', '10-Q']) list of strings. Default: all form types

ciks.description: (optional) Filters results to filings from specified CIKs, type list of strings. Leading zeros are optional but may be included. Example: [ "0001811414", "1318605" ]. Default: all CIKs

start_date.description: (optional) Start date for the search range in yyyy-mm-dd format. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

end_date.description: (optional) End date for the search range, in the same format as startDate. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

page.description: (optional) Used for pagination. Each page contains up to 100 matching filings. Increase the page number to retrieve the next set of 100 filings. Example: 3 retrieves the third page. Default: 1

top_n_results.description: (optional) Return only the first N results out of 100 from the page. If not provided, all 100 results will be returned. E.g. if page is 2, and top_n_results is 10, you will receive results 100 to 110.

function.name: calculator
function.description: Evaluate a mathematical expression and return the result. Use this tool for all arithmetic calculations instead of computing by hand. Supports: +, -, *, /, ** (exponentiation), % (modulo), and parentheses for grouping. Available functions: abs(), min(), max(), sqrt(), log(), log10(). Examples: '(5000 - 3200) * 0.21', '(2865507 / 1905871) ** 0.5 - 1', '14060 / 2148'.

function.name: price_history
function.description: Fetch historical daily price data for a specific asset class. Returns a CSV table with one row per day. Use asset_class='equity' or 'etf' for US-listed stocks/ETFs (e.g. AAPL, SPY), 'crypto' for pairs like btcusd (lowercase, no dash), or 'fx' for pairs like audusd. Non-US equities and most indices/futures are not covered by the pricing provider. Each row includes raw OHLC (open/high/low/close), split- and dividend-adjusted OHLC (adjOpen/adjHigh/adjLow/adjClose), raw and adjusted volume, divCash (dividend amount on the date), and splitFactor.

ticker.description: Ticker symbol. High-level symbology rules: (1) Equity/ETF/mutual fund: use the US exchange-listed symbol (AAPL, SPY, VFIAX). Symbols are case-insensitive and contain only letters and dashes. (2) Share classes use a dash, not a dot: 'BRK-A' / 'BRK-B' (not 'BRK.A'), 'BF-B'. (3) Securities are identified by the ticker as listed on the primary US exchange; non-US listings, OTC pink sheets, indices, and futures are generally not covered. (4) Tickers can be reused after delisting; data is point-in-time for the entity that held the ticker on each date. (5) Crypto: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'btcusd', 'ethusd'. (6) FX: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'audusd', 'eurusd'.

asset_class.description: Asset class used to route to the proper pricing endpoint.

function.name: submit_final_result
function.description: Submits the final answer to the user. You should include your final answer, as well as any necessary reasoning, justification, calculations, and explanation. Finally, you should provide any sources used to answer the question. You MUST use this tool to submit your final result. The user will not see your response if you do not use this tool to submit. You will not be able to continue working after this tool is called; the conversation will be ended.

final_result.description: The final result to submit to the user

steps.message:
You are an IPO and financial diligence agent.

You are given a question and must answer it using the tools provided.

You cannot interact with the user or ask clarifying questions.

Answer solely based on available information and tools.

Assume the current date is May 31, 2026.

---

TOOLS AND DATA SOURCES

You have access to a document storage system.

You may store parsed HTML retrieved from the web and later retrieve
information using LLM-based retrieval prompts.

Use this system to avoid context-window limitations.

When working with long filings, prefer section-specific retrieval prompts
rather than broad document-level retrieval.

Examples:

- retrieve the Segment Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation table from MD&A
- retrieve the glossary definition of Starshield
- retrieve the controlled-company governance disclosure

For peer-comparison questions:

- retrieve peer SEC filings using edgar_search
- do not rely on memory for peer financial figures

For historical public-equity price data not available in SEC filings:

- use price_history as primary source
- use raw unadjusted prices unless adjusted prices are requested
- fall back to web_search only if price_history fails

Stock indices are not covered by price_history.

Use authoritative sources such as FRED or web_search for index levels.

---

SOURCE HIERARCHY

Prioritize sources in this order:

1. S-1 / S-1A filings
2. Incorporated SEC filings
3. SEC exhibits and merger documents
4. SEC periodic filings
5. Company earnings materials
6. External sources

If figures differ across sources:

prefer the most authoritative SEC source containing the directly reported
figure and explain material discrepancies when relevant.

SEC filings are the primary and most authoritative source of financial and
disclosure information.

---

ANSWER STANDARDS

You must call submit_final_result with the completed answer.

Your submission will not be processed unless submit_final_result is called.

Include:

- complete reasoning
- calculations
- supporting analysis

You will be evaluated on:

- final-answer accuracy
- analytical correctness

Do not round intermediate calculations.

Round only final reported values.

When possible, report calculated figures to at least two decimal places.

Report financial figures using the same scale and units used in the filing
unless otherwise specified.

Share prices should be reported with two decimal places.

---

DISCLOSURE DISCIPLINE

Before reaching a conclusion:

determine whether the filing provides sufficient information to answer:

- directly
- partially
- only through inference

Distinguish carefully among:

- facts explicitly disclosed
- reasonable filing-supported inferences
- information not disclosed

Do not speculate beyond what the filing supports.

If material information is absent:

explicitly state that the filing does not provide sufficient information to
conclude.

Absence of disclosure may itself be analytically material.

---

FORWARD-LOOKING STATEMENTS

Treat separately:

- historical facts
- roadmap targets
- commercialization expectations
- management intentions
- forward-looking statements

Do not present aspirations or plans as historical outcomes.

---

GOVERNANCE AND LEGAL QUESTIONS

For governance, legal, or disclosure-language questions:

use the filing's precise terminology whenever possible.

Distinguish among:

- requirements
- exemptions
- company elections
- forward-looking language

---

ACCOUNTING POLICY QUESTIONS

For accounting-policy questions:

1. Identify the relevant disclosed accounting policy or treatment
2. Determine whether the filing explicitly resolves the issue
3. If unresolved, state that the filing does not specify treatment

---

NON-GAAP QUESTIONS

For non-GAAP measures:

identify:

- the starting GAAP metric
- each disclosed adjustment
- whether economically recurring costs are excluded

Do not accept non-GAAP measures without analyzing their construction.

---

AUDIENCE TAGS

Questions may include audience tags such as:

[PUBLIC | BANK]
[VC | CREDIT]
[LEGAL | ACCOUNTING]

Use these tags to calibrate terminology and analytical emphasis while
remaining grounded in disclosed facts.

---

SOURCES

At the end of your answer provide:

{
  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "...",
      "name": "..."
    }
  ]
}


---


FILING CONTEXT

The primary filing for this benchmark is the SpaceX S-1 filed May 20, 2026:

https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm

Important:

The consolidated financial statements have been retrospectively recast under
common-control accounting to include:

- xAI (merged February 2, 2026)
- X Holdings (merged into xAI March 28, 2025)

for all historical periods presented.

No period reflects standalone SpaceX operations exclusively.

When interpreting historical segment figures, state whether they reflect the
recast consolidated entity.

SpaceX (SPCX) has no public trading history as of May 30, 2026.

Do not attempt price_history retrieval for SpaceX or SPCX.

For peer valuation or peer financial benchmarking, retrieve peer-company SEC
filings using edgar_search rather than relying on memory.

arguments.prompt: What does the SpaceX S-1 filing disclose about Connectivity revenue? Include all definitions, descriptions, components, and metrics related to Connectivity revenue. Look for both consumer broadband (Starlink) and enterprise/government applications. {{spacex_s1}}

What are the revenue segments disclosed in the SpaceX S-1 filing? Provide details on each segment including Launch, Space Systems, Connectivity, and any other segments. Include segment definitions and revenue figures. {{spacex_s1}}

results.content:
Based on the provided SpaceX S-1 filing excerpts, here is a comprehensive breakdown of the disclosures regarding Connectivity revenue, including definitions, descriptions, components, metrics, and financial performance.

### 1. Overview and Definitions
The **Connectivity segment** operates a worldwide high-speed, low-latency broadband network powered by thousands of Starlink satellites in Low-Earth Orbit (LEO). It delivers connectivity to millions of consumer, enterprise, and government customers across 156 markets (164 countries, territories, and other markets) as of March 31, 2026. 

The Connectivity business is divided into three main components, plus a mobile-specific offering:
*   **Starlink Consumer Broadband:** The world’s largest and most advanced space-based internet broadband service, providing fiber-like download speeds globally.
*   **Enterprise Solutions:** High-speed, low-latency internet services offered to a wide array of enterprises across industries including construction, agriculture, retail, telecom, hospitality, aviation, maritime, and land mobility. 
*   **Government Solutions:** High-speed, resilient connectivity for government customers via Starshield, a secure satellite network designed specifically for government use.
*   **Starlink Mobile:** Satellite-to-mobile services enabling consumers, businesses, and public-sector customers to use existing phones in more places. *Note: Revenue from Starlink Mobile service offerings is included within the "Enterprise & Government" revenue line item.*

### 2. Revenue Generation Models
The Connectivity segment generates revenue primarily through (i) broadband and mobile connectivity services and (ii) the sale of the Starlink Kit (inclusive of the terminal). 

**Consumer Broadband Revenue:**
*   Generated primarily through monthly subscription fees based on geographic market and download speed, plus typically a one-time upfront terminal cost (Starlink Kit).
*   Broadband contracts are generally month-to-month, and revenue recognized for recurring consumer customers is equal to the amount billed in that month.

**Enterprise Revenue:**
*   Generated through contracts structured as a combination of subscriptions, data consumption, and capacity, or on a percentage-of-completion basis, depending on customer needs.
*   Starlink Kit revenue for enterprise customers is recognized upon delivery or installation.

**Government Revenue (Starshield):**
*   Generated via long-term contracts for Starshield.
*   Revenue is recognized over time for multi-year contracts using the cost-to-cost input method (recording revenue based on costs incurred to date relative to total estimated cost).

**Starlink Mobile Revenue:**
*   SpaceX charges Mobile Network Operators (MNOs) either a fixed fee or a per-mobile user fee-based amount, which is typically passed through to the customer via the carrier. 
*   Mobile connectivity agreements are generally multi-year contractual obligations ranging from one to five years, though the customer can generally terminate at any time.

### 3. Revenue Recognition Policies
*   **Multiple Performance Obligations:** Substantially all contracts with Starlink customers contain multiple performance obligations: (i) broadband services and (ii) the Starlink Kit. The transaction price is allocated to each obligation based on its standalone selling price. Starlink Mobile services have a single performance obligation.
*   **Service Recognition:** Performance obligations for broadband and mobile services are satisfied over time as the customer simultaneously receives and consumes the benefits. 
*   **Pricing Structures:** Service revenue is generated via (i) fixed-price services requiring advance or recurring monthly payments, or (ii) variable-priced services based on actual data consumption. 
*   **Deferred Revenue:** Advanced payments for broadband and mobile services are included in deferred revenue and recognized either ratably over the subscription term or based on actual data consumption. Deferred revenue primarily relates to Space agreements and Connectivity enterprise/government contracts. Total deferred revenue was $10,179 million as of Dec 31, 2024 (with $4,080M recognized as revenue in 2025), growing to $12,116 million as of Dec 31, 2025.

### 4. Key Operating and Performance Metrics
**Subscriber Metrics:**
*   **Starlink Subscribers:** Approximately 10.3 million as of March 31, 2026, up approximately 105% from 5.0 million a year prior. Subscribers are reported on a per-Service Line basis, meaning individual end-users are likely meaningfully higher.
*   **Starlink Mobile:** Approximately 7.4 million monthly unique devices across approximately 30 countries as of March 31, 2026.
*   **ARPU (Average Revenue Per User):** Starlink Subscriber ARPU declined 11.2% in 2025 and 22.9% in Q1 2026, primarily due to international expansion and the addition of lower-priced service plans.

**Network and Technology Metrics (as of March 31, 2026):**
*   **Satellite Constellation:** Approximately 9,600 Starlink broadband and mobile satellites in LEO (~75% of all active maneuverable satellites in orbit). This includes approximately 650 V1 Mobile satellites.
*   **Service Quality:** Median latency of ~25 milliseconds and median download speeds of 225 Mbps during peak hours for residential users.
*   **Future Capacity:** V3 satellites (expected 2H 2026) will offer 1 Tbps of downlink capacity per satellite. A single Starship launch can deploy up to 60 V3 satellites, a potential twenty-fold increase in downlink capacity relative to a Falcon 9 launch.

### 5. Cost of Revenue Components
The Connectivity segment’s cost of revenue includes:
*   Depreciation (inclusive of launch, satellite, and ground infrastructure costs)
*   Starlink Kit costs
*   Shipping and handling costs
*   Ground operating expenses
*   Employee compensation costs (salaries, benefits, share-based compensation for engineering and operations teams)
*   Payment processor fees
*   Warranty expense
*   Inventory excess and obsolescence
*   Customs and duties

### 6. Financial Performance and Growth Drivers
**Total Connectivity Revenue Growth:**
*   **2024:** Revenue increased by $3,730 million compared to 2023, driven by growth in Starlink consumer subscribers and Connectivity enterprise/government sales.
*   **2025:** Revenue increased by $3,788 million (49.8%) compared to 2024. This was driven by a $2,377 million increase from consumer subscribers (99.9% subscriber growth, offset by 11.2% ARPU decline) and a $1,411 million increase from enterprise and government customers. 
*   **Q1 2026:** Revenue increased by $782 million (31.6%) compared to Q1 2025. Driven by $656 million from consumer subscribers (104.7% subscriber growth, offset by 22.9% ARPU decline) and $126 million from enterprise and government. 

**Enterprise & Government Revenue Breakdown (2025 vs 2024):**
*   Enterprise connectivity business grew by $1,218 million (inclusive of $632 million growth in the mobile connectivity business).
*   Government connectivity business grew by $193 million.

**Enterprise & Government Revenue Breakdown (Q1 2026 vs Q1 2025):**
*   Aviation, maritime, and other enterprise business grew by $209 million.
*   Mobile connectivity business grew by $85 million.
*   Government connectivity business decreased by $175 million.

**Profitability:**
*   **Connectivity Income from Operations:** Increased to $4,423 million in 2025 (up $2,417 million from 2024) and $1,188 million in Q1 2026 (up $155 million from Q1 2025). Profitability was offset by higher depreciation of capitalized launch/satellite costs, marketing, and international expansion costs.
*   **Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA:** Increased to $7,168 million in 2025 (up from $3,849 million in 2024) and $2,087 million in Q1 2026 (up from $1,618 million in Q1 2025).

### 7. Total Addressable Market (TAM)
SpaceX estimates a total Connectivity market opportunity of **$1.6 trillion**, reflecting primarily consumer use cases. This is broken down into:
*   **Starlink Broadband:** ~$870 billion opportunity (driven by an estimated 1.8 billion global households in 2025 and 3.3 billion potential end users in currently served markets).
*   **Starlink Mobile:** ~$740 billion opportunity.
*   Additional opportunities exist in enterprise and government applications as connectivity evolves into a critical infrastructure layer for autonomous systems, transportation, and smart devices.

# SpaceX S-1 Revenue Segments

SpaceX discloses **three reportable operating segments** — **Space**, **Connectivity**, and **AI** — with further disaggregation of revenue by type within each segment. Below is a detailed breakdown of each segment, its definition, sub-categories, and revenue figures.

---

## 1. Space Segment

### Definition
The Space segment designs, manufactures, and launches reusable rockets (Falcon 9, Falcon Heavy, Dragon spacecraft, and the in-development Starship) to provide high-cadence, reliable, and affordable access to space at unprecedented scale. Revenue is derived from fixed-price contracts related to the development and provision of launch services for both commercial customers and governmental agency space programs. The segment flies to LEO, MEO, GEO, lunar, and interplanetary trajectories, as well as the International Space Station.

Space segment revenue **only reflects customer launches and customer activities**. For launches of SpaceX's own Starlink satellites, no inter-segment revenue is recognized; those launch costs are capitalized within the Connectivity segment as property, plant, and equipment.

### Sub-Categories and Revenue Recognition

| Sub-Category | Recognition Method | Contract Duration | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Launch Services** | Point in time (upon launch or deployment of customer's payload to intended orbit) | 1–5 years | Fixed-price contracts for commercial and government launch missions. Revenue and costs are deferred until launch/deployment due to interchangeability of flight hardware and minimal unique engineering costs. |
| **Launch & Development** | Over time (cost-to-cost input method) | 1–14 years | Fixed-price contracts for development of spacecraft and provision of launch and mission services for government agency space programs (utilizing Falcon 9, Falcon Heavy, Starship, and Dragon). Performance creates an asset with no alternative use and the Company has an enforceable right to payment. |

### Revenue Figures

| Space Sub-Category | FY 2023 | FY 2024 | FY 2025 | Q1 2025 | Q1 2026 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Launch Services | $1,964M | $2,584M | $2,576M | $566M | $330M |
| Launch & Development | $1,593M | $1,212M | $1,510M | $299M | $289M |
| **Space Total** | **$3,557M** | **$3,796M** | **$4,086M** | **$865M** | **$619M** |

### Mix as % of Space Segment Revenue

| | Q1 2026 | FY 2025 | FY 2024 | FY 2023 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Launch Services | 53.3% | 63.0% | 68.2% | 55.2% |
| Launch & Development | 46.7% | 37.0% | 31.8% | 44.8% |

SpaceX expects Launch & Development to represent a larger portion of Space revenue over time as it continues to serve long-term government contracts. Space revenue growth is expected to remain lower than total company revenue growth as internal businesses (Connectivity and, in the future, AI) absorb most of the growth in launch capacity.

### Segment Profitability (FY 2025)
- Loss from operations: **$(657) million**
- Segment Adjusted EBITDA: **$653 million**
- R&D expense funded for Starship program: **$3,004 million**

### Segment Profitability (Q1 2026)
- Loss from operations: **$(662) million**
- Segment Adjusted EBITDA: **$(351) million**
- R&D expense for Starship: **$930 million**

---

## 2. Connectivity Segment

### Definition
The Connectivity segment operates a worldwide high-speed, low-latency broadband data and communications network powered by over 9,600 Starlink broadband and mobile satellites in Low-Earth Orbit, delivering connectivity to millions of consumer, enterprise, and government customers across 164 countries, territories, and other markets. The segment generates revenue from (i) broadband and mobile connectivity services provided through Starlink and (ii) the sale of the Starlink Kit (inclusive of the terminal). **All of the company's products revenue is attributable to the Connectivity segment.**

Service revenue is recognized over time as the customer simultaneously receives and consumes the benefits. Revenue is generated from fixed-price services (advance or recurring monthly payments) or variable-priced services based on actual data consumption. Advanced payments are recognized either ratably over the subscription term or based on usage.

### Sub-Categories

| Sub-Category | Description |
|---|---|
| **Consumer** | Starlink Consumer Broadband — the world's largest and most advanced space-based internet broadband service, providing fiber-like download speeds (median of 225 Mbps during peak hours for residential users as of March 31, 2026) with technological capability to provide service everywhere on Earth, including the poles. |
| **Enterprise & Government** | Enterprise Solutions, Government contracts, and **Starlink Mobile** service offerings (satellite-to-mobile texting and voice services to mobile users). Per footnote (1): *"Enterprise & Government revenue includes revenue from Starlink Mobile service offerings."* |

### Revenue Figures

| Connectivity Sub-Category | FY 2023 | FY 2024 | FY 2025 | Q1 2025 | Q1 2026 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Consumer | $2,817M | $4,830M | $7,208M | $1,492M | $2,148M |
| Enterprise & Government | $1,052M | $2,769M | $4,179M | $983M | $1,109M |
| **Connectivity Total** | **$3,869M** | **$7,599M** | **$11,387M** | **$2,475M** | **$3,257M** |

### Segment Profitability (FY 2025)
- Income from operations: **$4,423 million**
- Segment Adjusted EBITDA: **$7,168 million**
- Year-over-year growth: Revenue +49.8%, Income from operations +120.4%, Segment Adjusted EBITDA +86.2%

### Segment Profitability (Q1 2026)
- Income from operations: **$1,188 million**
- Segment Adjusted EBITDA: **$2,087 million**

---

## 3. AI Segment

### Definition
The AI segment operates a vertically integrated AI platform spanning:
- **Grok** — a truth-seeking frontier large language model (LLM)
- **AI solutions for consumer and enterprise customers** — including Grok Business and Grok Enterprise, sold on license-, consumption-, or outcome-based pricing models
- **X** — a real-time information, entertainment, and free speech platform
- **AI computational infrastructure** — AI hosting and compute capacity

SpaceX plans to prioritize growth and investment in this segment to capture significant opportunities in AI applications and compute infrastructure, and expects to allocate a significant amount of launch capacity to the AI segment in the future.

### Sub-Categories

| Sub-Category | Description |
|---|---|
| **Advertising** | Revenue from advertising sales on the X platform. |
| **AI Solutions & Infrastructure** | Revenue from X subscriptions, data licensing, Grok AI solutions (consumer and enterprise), and AI computational infrastructure services. |

### Revenue Figures

| AI Sub-Category | FY 2023 | FY 2024 | FY 2025 | Q1 2025 | Q1 2026 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Advertising | $2,323M | $1,728M | $1,844M | $443M | $343M |
| AI Solutions & Infrastructure | $638M | $892M | $1,357M | $284M | $475M |
| **AI Total** | **$2,961M** | **$2,620M** | **$3,201M** | **$727M** | **$818M** |

### Segment Profitability (FY 2025)
- Revenue: **$3,201 million**
- Loss from operations: **$(2,469) million** (referenced in Q1 2026 context; full-year figure implied)
- Segment Adjusted EBITDA: **$(609) million** (Q1 2026 figure)

### Segment Profitability (Q1 2026)
- Revenue: **$818 million**
- Loss from operations: **$(2,469) million**
- Segment Adjusted EBITDA: **$(609) million**

---

## Total Consolidated Revenue

| | FY 2023 | FY 2024 | FY 2025 | Q1 2025 | Q1 2026 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Total Revenues** | **$10,387M** | **$14,015M** | **$18,674M** | **$4,067M** | **$4,694M** |

### Year-over-Year Revenue Growth Drivers

**FY 2025 vs. FY 2024** — Revenue increased by **$4,659 million (+33.2%)**:
- Connectivity segment: +$3,788 million (Starlink subscriber base growth + enterprise/government sales)
- Space segment: +$290 million (increases in Launch and Development)

**FY 2024 vs. FY 2023** — Revenue increased by **$3,628 million (+34.9%)**:
- Connectivity segment: +$3,730 million (Starlink consumer subscriber growth + enterprise/government sales)
- Space segment: +$239 million (increase in Falcon 9 launches, partially offset by decrease in Launch & Development due to timing of government contracts)
- AI segment: −$341 million (decrease in advertising sales, partially offset by increase in X subscriptions and data licensing)

---

## Deferred Revenue

Deferred revenue is recorded when cash payments are received or due in advance of the Company's performance. It **primarily relates to Space agreements and Connectivity enterprise and government contracts**.

| Metric | Amount |
|---|---|
| Total deferred revenue as of December 31, 2024 | **$10,179 million** |
| Portion recognized as revenue during FY 2025 | **$4,080 million** |
| Total deferred revenue as of December 31, 2025 | **$12,116 million** |

---

## Capital Expenditures by Segment

| Segment | FY 2025 | Q1 2026 |
|---|---|---|
| Space | $3,832M | $1,052M |
| Connectivity | $4,178M | $1,332M |
| AI | *(not disclosed in chunk)* | $7,723M |

---

## Summary of Segment Definitions (from Notes to Consolidated Financial Statements)

> Space Exploration Technologies Corp. and its wholly owned subsidiaries, collectively referred to as the "Company" or "SpaceX," operate three segments – **(i) the Space segment** designs, manufactures, and launches reusable rockets to provide high cadence, reliable, and affordable access to space at unprecedented scale, **(ii) the Connectivity segment** operates a worldwide high-speed, low-latency broadband network powered by thousands of Starlink satellites in Low-Earth Orbit, delivering connectivity to millions of consumer, enterprise, and government customers through our Starlink offering, and **(iii) the AI segment** operates a vertically integrated AI platform spanning a frontier LLM Grok, AI solutions for consumer and enterprise customers, X — a real-time information, entertainment, and free speech platform — and AI computational infrastructure.

# Connectivity Segment: Disclosed Metrics and KPIs

## 1. Subscriber Metrics

### Starlink Subscribers (Broadband)
| Period | Subscribers (millions) | YoY Growth |
|---|---|---|
| Q1 2026 | 10.3 | 105% |
| Q1 2025 | 5.0 | 91% |
| FY 2025 | 8.9 | — |
| FY 2024 | 4.4 | — |
| FY 2023 | 2.3 | — |

**Definition:** A Starlink Subscriber is a unique **Service Line** directly assigned to a Starlink.com account registered to a person or entity that does **not** have a direct, negotiated agreement with the Starlink sales team. A Service Line is an individual instance of Starlink broadband internet service provisioned under a subscription plan, generally associated with a specific terminal or group of terminals.

- **Includes:** Personal plans (Residential, Roam) and Business plans (Local Priority, Global Priority).
- **Excludes:** Managed enterprise and government customers with contracts in aviation, maritime, land mobility, fixed sites, and government entities.
- An individual, household, or business may maintain multiple Service Lines (e.g., a Residential line and a separate Roam line count as two subscribers). Conversely, multiple end users may share a single Service Line, meaning the number of individual end users is "likely meaningfully higher than 10.3 million."

### Starlink Mobile (Satellite-to-Mobile) Devices
- **~7.4 million monthly unique devices** as of March 31, 2026, across **~30 countries**.
- Services include light data, SMS texting, and over-the-top voice services (e.g., WhatsApp, FaceTime).
- Partnerships with **~30 MNOs** on six continents, covering an area home to **~1.9 billion people**.
- Revenue model: MNOs charged either a fixed fee or a per-mobile user fee-based amount, typically passed through to the end customer via the carrier.

---

## 2. ARPU Metrics

### Starlink Subscriber ARPU (Broadband)
| Period | ARPU ($/month) | YoY Change |
|---|---|---|
| Q1 2026 | $66 | −22.9% |
| Q1 2025 | $86 | — |
| FY 2025 | $81 | −11.2% |
| FY 2024 | $91 | −8.1% |
| FY 2023 | $99 | — |

**Definition:** Service revenue generated from Starlink Subscribers during the period ÷ (average number of Starlink Subscribers during the period × number of months in the period).

**Trend & Outlook:**
- ARPU has declined every year since 2023, driven primarily by **international expansion** and the **addition of lower-priced service plans**.
- The company explicitly states its strategy is focused on **driving sustainable revenue growth and expanding margins through operational efficiencies and technological advancements, rather than prioritizing increases in ARPU**.
- ARPU is **expected to continue declining** over the next few years as:
  - The portion of the subscriber base outside North America grows.
  - Lower-priced service plans are added.
  - Monthly service plan fees for broadband offerings are adjusted.
- These declines are expected to be **offset by increased scale** and technological advancement in launch, satellite, and user terminal operations.

### Starlink Mobile ARPU (Assumptions, Not Actuals)
The company discloses **region-specific ARPU assumptions** (not actual reported figures) for market sizing purposes:

| Market Tier | Weighted Avg. Monthly Mobile ARPU |
|---|---|
| High-income markets | $18 |
| Upper-middle income markets | $5 |
| Lower-middle income markets | $2 |
| Low-income markets | $2 |
| **Global weighted average** | **$8** |

Based on these assumptions, the estimated Starlink Mobile market opportunity is **$740 billion**.

---

## 3. Satellite & Network Operating Metrics

### Satellite Constellation
- **~9,600 Starlink broadband and mobile satellites** in LEO as of March 31, 2026.
- This represents **~75% of all active maneuverable satellites in orbit**.
- **V1 Mobile constellation:** ~650 V1 Mobile satellites dedicated to satellite-to-mobile capabilities (began deploying January 2024).
- **V3 next-generation satellites:** Designed to offer **1 Tbps of downlink capacity per satellite**; deployment expected to commence in H2 2026 using Starship. A single Starship launch can deploy up to **60 V3 satellites**, representing a potential **twenty-fold increase** in downlink capacity deployed relative to a Falcon 9 launch.

### Broadband Performance
- **Median latency:** ~25 milliseconds as of March 31, 2026.
- **Median download speed:** 225 Mbps during peak hours for residential users as of March 31, 2026.
- Technological capability to provide service everywhere on Earth, including the poles.

### Geographic Coverage
- **164 countries, territories, and other markets** for broadband.
- Collectively home to **more than 3.3 billion people**.
- Starlink Mobile available across **~30 countries**.

---

## 4. Segment Financial Metrics

### Segment Income from Operations
| Period | Income from Operations ($M) |
|---|---|
| Q1 2026 | $1,188 |
| Q1 2025 | $1,033 |
| FY 2025 | $4,423 |

### Segment Adjusted EBITDA

| FY 2025 | $7,168 |

| FY 2023 | $1,602 |

---

## 5. Revenue Breakdown (from MD&A Narrative)

### Q1 2026 vs. Q1 2025
- Total revenue increased **$782 million (31.6%)**.
- **Consumer subscriber revenue:** +$656 million (driven by 104.7% subscriber growth, offset by 22.9% ARPU decline).
- **Enterprise & government revenue:** +$126 million:
  - Aviation, maritime, and other enterprise: +$209 million

### FY 2025 vs. FY 2024
- Total revenue increased **$3,788 million (49.8%)**.
- **Consumer subscriber revenue:** +$2,377 million (99.9% subscriber growth, offset by 11.2% ARPU decline).
- **Enterprise & government revenue:** +$1,411 million:
  - Enterprise connectivity: +$1,218 million (including mobile connectivity +$632 million)
  - Government connectivity: +$193 million

### FY 2024 vs. FY 2023
- Total revenue increased **$3,730 million (96.4%)**.
- **Consumer subscriber revenue:** +$2,013 million (96.5% subscriber growth, offset by 8.1% ARPU decline).
- **Enterprise & government revenue:** +$1,717 million:
  - Enterprise connectivity: +$466 million
  - Government connectivity: +$1,250 million

---

## 6. Cost & Expense Drivers (Q1 2026 vs. Q1 2025)

### Cost of Revenue: +$437 million (36.0%)
| Driver | Amount ($M) |

---

## 7. Enterprise Customer Retention
- Since 2023, **no Starlink Enterprise customer contributing more than $750,000 of annual revenue has voluntarily discontinued service**, despite the ability to cancel at any time.

---

## 8. Revenue Model Summary
| Customer Type | Revenue Model |
|---|---|
| Consumer | Monthly subscription fees (based on geography and speed) + one-time Starlink Kit sale; revenue recognized ratably over service period |
| Enterprise | Combination of subscriptions, data consumption, and capacity contracts; recognized over time or on percentage-of-completion basis |
| Government | Long-term contracts for Starshield (secure, dedicated satellite network for U.S. Government and national security) |
| MNOs (Mobile) | Fixed fee or per-mobile user fee-based amount, typically passed through to customer via carrier |

## Metrics NOT Disclosed

The following metrics are **not disclosed** for the Connectivity segment:

### Subscriber-Level Metrics

# Connectivity Revenue Breakdown and Deferred Revenue Analysis

## 1. Service vs. Products Revenue Breakdown

The filing explicitly states: **"All of products revenue is attributable to the Connectivity segment."** This means the company-wide products/services disaggregation can be used to derive the Connectivity split:

**Three Months Ended March 31, 2026:**
| Component | Amount ($M) |
|---|---|
| Products (Starlink Kit sales) | $380 |
| Services (broadband, mobile, enterprise/government) | $2,877 |
| **Total Connectivity** | **$3,257** |

**Three Months Ended March 31, 2025:**
| Component | Amount ($M) |
|---|---|
| Products (Starlink Kit sales) | $352 |
| Services (broadband, mobile, enterprise/government) | $2,123 |
| **Total Connectivity** | **$2,475** |

*(Services figures derived as: Total Connectivity revenue minus Products revenue, since all products revenue flows through Connectivity.)*

For the full year 2025, total Connectivity revenue was **$11,387 million** (vs. $7,599 million in 2024 and $3,869 million in 2023), though the annual products/services split is not separately tabulated in the excerpts provided.

Within the Services category, the filing further disaggregates Connectivity revenue by customer type:

| Connectivity Sub-segment | FY 2025 ($M) | FY 2024 ($M) | FY 2023 ($M) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Consumer | 7,208 | 4,830 | 2,817 |
| Enterprise & Government (incl. Starlink Mobile) | 4,179 | 2,769 | 1,052 |
| **Total Connectivity** | **11,387** | **7,599** | **3,869** |

---

## 2. Geographic Revenue Breakdown

**No specific geographic revenue breakdown is disclosed** in the provided excerpts. The filing references that Connectivity services are delivered "throughout 156 markets" and discusses ARPU declines "primarily due to international expansion and the addition of lower priced service plans," but it does not present a quantitative revenue disaggregation by geography (e.g., domestic vs. international, or by region). The only geographic color provided is qualitative:

- Starlink Subscriber ARPU declined **11.2%** in FY 2025 and **22.9%** in Q1 2026, attributed to international expansion into lower-priced markets.
- Starlink Mobile partnerships span "approximately 30 MNOs on six continents, covering an area that is home to approximately 1.9 billion people."

---

## 3. Deferred Revenue Related to Connectivity

The filing does **not** disclose a standalone dollar figure for deferred revenue attributable solely to Connectivity. Instead, it states that deferred revenue **"primarily relates to Space agreements and Connectivity enterprise and government contracts"** — bundling the two segments together without separate quantification.

The aggregate deferred revenue balances disclosed are:

| Date | Total Deferred Revenue ($M) | Amount Recognized as Revenue |
|---|---|---|
| December 31, 2024 | $10,179 | $4,080 recognized during FY 2025 |
| December 31, 2025 | $12,116 | $1,165 recognized during Q1 2026 |
| March 31, 2026 | $13,236 | — |

The filing clarifies that the Connectivity-related portion of deferred revenue arises specifically from **Connectivity enterprise and government contracts** (not consumer broadband), because:

- Consumer broadband contracts are "generally month-to-month" with revenue recognized equal to the amount billed each month, limiting advance-payment deferrals.
- Mobile connectivity agreements are "generally multi-year contractual obligations that range from one to five years," generating more significant deferred revenue.
- Starshield (government) contracts use the cost-to-cost input method over multi-year periods, contributing to deferred revenue.
- Starlink Kit (product) revenue is recognized at a point in time upon delivery/installation, so it does **not** generate deferred revenue.

The broader backlog of **$27,621 million** as of March 31, 2026 (of which $13,236 million was recognized as deferred revenue) provides additional context, with approximately 36% expected to be recognized within one year, 46% between one and three years, and 18% thereafter — but again, no Connectivity-specific carve-out is provided within these figures.

Based on the provided S-1 text, here are the definitions and details regarding Starlink's operational metrics and customer categories:

**1. Starlink Subscriber**
A "Starlink Subscriber" is defined as a unique Service Line that is directly assigned to a Starlink.com account registered to a person or entity that *does not* have a direct, negotiated agreement with the Starlink sales team. The count includes both Personal subscription plans (e.g., Residential and Roam) and Business subscription plans (e.g., Local Priority and Global Priority). 

**2. Service Line**
A "Service Line" is defined as an individual instance of Starlink broadband internet service provisioned under a subscription plan. It is generally associated with a specific Starlink terminal or group of terminals and is billed according to Starlink’s service plans and terms of service. The number of Service Lines is distinct from the number of unique devices, account holders, end users, or physical persons, as multiple people may share a single Service Line. Conversely, a single account holder may maintain multiple Service Lines (e.g., a Residential line and a separate Roam line), which would be counted as two separate Starlink Subscribers.

**3. ARPU (Starlink Subscriber ARPU)**
The company calculates ARPU as the service revenue generated from Starlink Subscribers during a given period, divided by:
*   (i) The average number of Starlink Subscribers during the period, and
*   (ii) The number of months in the period.

**4. Specific Exclusions from the Starlink Subscriber Count**
The Starlink Subscriber count specifically **excludes managed enterprise and government customers** with contracts in domains including aviation, maritime, land mobility, fixed sites, and government entities. (Additionally, by definition, any entity that has a direct, negotiated agreement with the Starlink sales team is excluded from this metric).

**5. Definition of Enterprise and Government Customers**
While not defined in a single glossary bullet point, the S-1 outlines Enterprise and Government customers functionally and by exclusion from the standard "Starlink Subscriber" base:
*   **Enterprise Customers:** These are entities that have direct, negotiated agreements with the Starlink sales team. They are offered high-speed, low-latency internet services across industries including construction, agriculture, retail, telecom, hospitality, aviation, maritime, and land mobility. This includes a broad fixed-site customer base (such as retail and financial services) that requires high availability for critical operations. Enterprise contracts are based on a combination of subscriptions, data consumption, capacity, or other custom pricing models.
*   **Government Customers:** These are entities provided with high-speed, resilient connectivity for public services, social impact, humanitarian efforts, and disaster response in remote and challenging environments. It also includes the use of "Starshield," which is a secure, dedicated satellite network designed specifically for United States Government customers and national security.

# SpaceX Revenue Disaggregation by Products and Services

Based on the revenue disaggregation table located in the revenue recognition note, the following amounts are reported for the fiscal years ended December 31 (in millions):

| | 2025 | 2024 | 2023 |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Products** | $1,510 | $1,470 | $1,093 |
| **Services** | $17,164 | $12,545 | $9,294 |
| **Total revenues** | $18,674 | $14,015 | $10,387 |

## Confirmation of Connectivity Attribution

Yes — the note explicitly states: **"All of products revenue is attributable to the Connectivity segment."** This applies across all three fiscal years presented (2023, 2024, and 2025). Products revenue (which consists of Starlink hardware kits sold to consumers, enterprise, and government customers) is entirely housed within the Connectivity segment, while services revenue spans all three reportable segments (Space, Connectivity, and AI).

Based on the provided financial disclosures, here is the information regarding the Starlink Kit:

**What is the Starlink Kit and how is it sold?**
*   **Definition:** The Starlink Kit refers to a set of hardware products needed to connect to the Starlink network, typically including a Starlink User Terminal (or terminal). 
*   **Sales Model:** The kit is sold to consumers, enterprise, and government customers. For consumers, it is typically sold as a one-time hardware purchase alongside a monthly subscription fee for broadband services. 
*   **Revenue Recognition:** Revenue for the Starlink Kit is recognized at a point in time—specifically, upon delivery to the customer, or upon installation for certain enterprise customers. The revenue is reported net of sales returns, credits, and chargebacks. Shipping and handling charges are included in the transaction price but are treated as fulfillment activities rather than a separate performance obligation.
*   **Warranty:** The company offers a standard product warranty for one to two years on Starlink Kits, though it notes that warranty expenses and related liabilities are immaterial to the consolidated financial statements.

**What is the relationship between Starlink Kit sales and Connectivity revenue?**
*   The Connectivity segment generates its revenue from two primary streams: (i) broadband and mobile connectivity services and (ii) the sale of the Starlink Kit. 
*   Substantially all Starlink customer contracts contain multiple performance obligations (the service and the kit). The company accounts for these as distinct obligations and allocates the transaction price to each based on their standalone selling prices. 
*   While service revenue is recognized ratably over the subscription term, the kit revenue is recognized upfront at the point of delivery or installation.

**Are there any disclosures about kit pricing, kit margins, or kit volumes?**
*   **Pricing:** The text does not disclose specific dollar amounts or average selling prices for the kits. It only states that the standalone selling price is determined based on the price at which the kit is sold separately to similar customers in similar locations.
*   **Margins:** No explicit gross margin percentages or dollar amounts for kit margins are disclosed. However, the filings note that "Starlink Kit costs" are a component of the Connectivity segment's cost of revenue. The company expects these costs to decrease as a percentage of revenue over time as it drives efficiencies in next-generation Starlink Kits. Additionally, the text mentions that a $39 million decrease in impairment charges in 2025 was related to the discontinuation of a Starlink Kit production line.
*   **Volumes:** Specific unit volumes (e.g., number of kits sold) are not disclosed. However, the MD&A references "higher kit deliveries" driving a $907 million increase in volume spend on Starlink Kits in 2024. It also notes R&D expense increases in 2025 ($22 million) and Q1 2026 ($8 million) specifically tied to the next-generation production development of Starlink Kits.

## Connectivity Revenue: Inferred Streams, Unquantified Components, and Modeling Limitations

### 1. Disclosure Architecture

The SpaceX S-1 discloses Connectivity revenue through two overlapping but non-aligned lenses:

**Lens A — Customer-type disaggregation (formal revenue table):**
| ($M) | FY2023 | FY2024 | FY2025 | Q1 2025 | Q1 2026 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Consumer | 2,817 | 4,830 | 7,208 | 1,492 | 2,148 |
| Enterprise & Government (incl. Starlink Mobile) | 1,052 | 2,769 | 4,179 | 983 | 1,109 |
| **Connectivity Total** | **3,869** | **7,599** | **11,387** | **2,475** | **3,257** |

**Lens B — Products vs. Services disaggregation (company-wide; all products = Connectivity):**
| ($M) | FY2023 | FY2024 | FY2025 | Q1 2025 | Q1 2026 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Products (Starlink Kit) | 1,093 | 1,470 | 1,510 | 352 | 380 |
| Connectivity Services (derived) | 2,776 | 6,129 | 9,877 | 2,123 | 2,877 |

The filing explicitly states: *"All of products revenue is attributable to the Connectivity segment."* This allows derivation of total Connectivity services revenue (Total Connectivity − Products), but the two lenses cannot be cross-tabulated because the customer-type split (Consumer vs. E&G) bundles service and kit revenue together within each line.

---

### 2. Revenue Streams That Can Be Reasonably Inferred

**a) Total Connectivity services revenue (derived with high confidence):**
Since all products revenue flows through Connectivity, Connectivity services = Total Connectivity − Products:
- FY2025: $11,387M − $1,510M = **$9,877M**
- FY2024: $7,599M − $1,470M = **$6,129M**
- FY2023: $3,869M − $1,093M = **$2,776M**
- Q1 2026: $3,257M − $380M = **$2,877M**
- Q1 2025: $2,475M − $352M = **$2,123M**

**b) Total Starlink Kit (products) revenue (directly disclosed):**
$1,510M (FY2025), $1,470M (FY2024), $1,093M (FY2023), $380M (Q1 2026), $352M (Q1 2025).

**c) Consumer service revenue (approximated via ARPU × subscribers, with caveats):**
The filing defines ARPU as: *Service revenue from Starlink Subscribers ÷ (average subscribers × months in period).* Starlink Subscribers exclude managed enterprise/government customers, so ARPU captures only consumer service revenue. Using period-end subscriber counts as a proxy for the average (under a linear-growth assumption):

| Period | ARPU | Avg Subs (est.) | Months | Est. Consumer Service | Consumer Line | Implied Consumer Kit |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| FY2025 | $81 | ~6.65M | 12 | ~$6,464M | $7,208M | ~$744M |
| FY2024 | $91 | ~3.35M | 12 | ~$3,658M | $4,830M | ~$1,172M |

This implies E&G Kit = Total Kit − Consumer Kit ≈ $766M (FY2025) and $298M (FY2024). These are reasonable inferences but depend on the undisclosed average subscriber count.

**d) Growth deltas for E&G sub-components (directly disclosed as deltas only):**
| Period | Enterprise (non-mobile) | Mobile | Government (Starshield) | Total E&G Δ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| FY2024 vs FY2023 | +$466M (incl. mobile) | not broken out | +$1,250M | +$1,717M |
| FY2025 vs FY2024 | +$586M (derived: $1,218M − $632M) | +$632M | +$193M | +$1,411M |
| Q1 2026 vs Q1 2025 | +$209M ("aviation, maritime, other") | +$85M | −$175M | +$126M |

---

### 3. Revenue Streams That Remain Unquantified

**a) Absolute Starlink Mobile revenue:** Only growth deltas are disclosed (+$632M in FY2025; +$85M in Q1 2026). V1 Mobile satellites began deploying January 2024, so the FY2023 base was likely near zero, but the FY2024 base is unknown. The 7.4M monthly unique devices metric is disclosed, but without a corresponding revenue figure, mobile ARPU cannot be calculated from actuals (only market-sizing assumptions of $2–$18/month by income tier are provided).

**b) Absolute Starshield / government revenue:** Only growth deltas are disclosed (+$1,250M in FY2024; +$193M in FY2025; −$175M in Q1 2026). The FY2023 base is unknown, so absolute government revenue cannot be chained forward. The Q1 2026 decline of $175M highlights the lumpy, contract-driven nature of this stream.

**c) Absolute aviation, maritime, land mobility, and fixed-site enterprise revenue:** These are referenced qualitatively (industries served include "construction, agriculture, retail, telecom, hospitality, aviation, maritime, and land mobility") but never quantified individually. The Q1 2026 narrative groups them as "aviation, maritime, and other enterprise business" with a +$209M delta, but no absolute level or prior-period comparable.

**d) Consumer Kit vs. E&G Kit split:** Can only be bounded, not determined. For Q1 2026, the ARPU × subscriber framework (under linear growth) implies negative E&G kit, which is impossible—indicating the average subscriber count exceeded the linear-growth estimate. The framework only approximately reconciles for annual periods and breaks down for Q1 2026.

**e) Geographic revenue split:** No quantitative domestic vs. international or regional breakdown is disclosed. ARPU decline is attributed qualitatively to "international expansion and the addition of lower-priced service plans," but the geographic revenue mix is not quantified.

**f) Connectivity-specific deferred revenue and backlog:** Total deferred revenue ($12,116M at Dec 31, 2025; $13,236M at Mar 31, 2026) and total backlog ($27,621M) are disclosed only at the consolidated level. The filing states deferred revenue "primarily relates to Space agreements and Connectivity enterprise and government contracts," but no Connectivity-specific carve-out is provided.

**g) Subscriber-level economics:** Churn (gross or net), customer acquisition cost, lifetime value, net subscriber additions, ARPU by plan type (Residential vs. Roam vs. Business Priority), and ARPU by geography (actuals) are all undisclosed.

**h) Kit unit economics:** Kit shipment volumes, average selling price, kit gross margin, and terminal manufacturing cost are not disclosed.

**i) Enterprise/government customer metrics:** Number of enterprise customers, number of government customers, average contract value, and revenue retention rates are not disclosed. (The filing does note that since 2023, no Enterprise customer contributing >$750K annual revenue has voluntarily discontinued service.)

---

### 4. Modeling Limitations Following from This Disclosure Structure

**Limitation 1 — Cannot decompose E&G into absolute sub-component levels.**
The narrative provides only year-over-year growth deltas for mobile, government, and enterprise sub-components, without absolute bases. Because the FY2023 starting split between enterprise and government within the $1,052M E&G total is unknown, absolute revenue levels for Starlink Mobile, Starshield, aviation, maritime, and other enterprise streams cannot be derived. A modeler cannot build separate revenue trajectories for these streams.

**Limitation 2 — Inconsistent sub-categorization across periods prevents clean chaining.**
The E&G sub-component taxonomy shifts across periods:
- FY2024 narrative: two buckets (enterprise connectivity, government connectivity)
- FY2025 narrative: three buckets, with mobile nested within enterprise ("inclusive of")
- Q1 2026 narrative: three buckets, with mobile broken out parallel to "aviation, maritime, and other enterprise"

These shifts mean growth deltas cannot be cleanly chained across periods to reconstruct absolute levels. Additionally, the Q1 2026 sub-components ($209M + $85M − $175M = $119M) do not precisely reconcile to the disclosed total E&G growth of $126M, leaving a ~$7M unexplained residual.

**Limitation 3 — Cannot separate hardware (kit) from service economics by customer type.**
The products/services split is disclosed only at the company-wide level (all products = Connectivity). The consumer vs. E&G split bundles kit and service together. Without kit volumes, ASP, or margins, a modeler cannot assess the hardware subsidy strategy, kit contribution margin, or the service-vs-hardware revenue mix by customer segment.

**Limitation 4 — ARPU × subscriber framework only approximately reconciles and breaks down for quarterly periods.**
The ARPU metric is defined using *average* subscribers, but only period-end subscriber counts are disclosed. Under a linear-growth assumption, the framework approximately reconciles to the Consumer line for annual periods (implying ~$744M consumer kit in FY2025), but produces impossible results (negative E&G kit) for Q1 2026. This means quarterly consumer service vs. kit splits cannot be reliably inferred.

**Limitation 5 — Cannot model geographic mix shifts quantitatively.**
ARPU has declined 33% from $99 (FY2023) to $66 (Q1 2026), attributed to international expansion and lower-priced plans. Without a geographic revenue split, a modeler cannot decompose ARPU decline into volume mix (geography) vs. price mix (plan tier) effects, limiting the ability to project future ARPU trajectories or model geographic expansion economics.

**Limitation 6 — Cannot assess subscriber retention or lifetime economics.**
No churn, CAC, LTV, or net retention metrics are disclosed. The Service Line definition (one subscriber = one service line, not one end user) further complicates cohort analysis. Without these, sustainable growth rates and subscriber lifetime value cannot be modeled.

**Limitation 7 — Cannot isolate government (Starshield) contract lumpiness.**
Government revenue declined $175M in Q1 2026 after growing $1,250M in FY2024 and $193M in FY2025. Without absolute levels, contract timing details, or backlog allocation, the volatility and forward visibility of this stream cannot be modeled. The cost-to-cost input method for Starshield means revenue recognition depends on cost incurrence patterns that are not separately disclosed.

**Limitation 8 — Cannot model Starlink Mobile unit economics separately.**
Mobile revenue is embedded within E&G, with only growth deltas disclosed. The 7.4M monthly unique devices metric and ~30 MNO partnerships are disclosed, but without a corresponding revenue figure, mobile ARPU, revenue per device, or MNO contract economics cannot be derived from actuals.

**Limitation 9 — Deferred revenue and backlog cannot be allocated to Connectivity.**
This limits forward revenue visibility for the Connectivity segment specifically. Since deferred revenue "primarily relates to Space agreements and Connectivity enterprise and government contracts," a significant portion of the $13,236M deferred revenue balance is Connectivity-related, but the exact amount and its expected recognition timing are not separable.

**Limitation 10 — Segment gross margin is not directly disclosed.**
Only segment income from operations ($4,423M in FY2025) and segment Adjusted EBITDA ($7,168M in FY2025) are disclosed. Without a separate cost-of-revenue line for the segment, gross margin cannot be calculated, limiting analysis of hardware vs. service cost structures and operating leverage.

---

### Summary

The Connectivity segment's disclosure structure supports confident derivation of **total services vs. products revenue** and **Consumer vs. E&G revenue** at the top line. It also supports **approximate** inference of consumer service vs. kit revenue via the ARPU × subscriber framework (for annual periods). However, the absolute revenue levels of **Starlink Mobile, Starshield/government, aviation, maritime, and other enterprise sub-streams** remain unquantified—only growth deltas are provided, and these deltas use inconsistent categorizations across periods that prevent clean reconstruction. The resulting modeling limitations are significant: a modeler cannot build bottom-up sub-segment revenue models, cannot separate hardware from service economics by customer type, cannot model geographic mix shifts quantitatively, cannot assess subscriber retention/lifetime economics, and cannot allocate deferred revenue or backlog to Connectivity. These gaps are particularly material for the E&G line, which grew from $1,052M (FY2023) to $4,179M (FY2025)—a 4× increase driven by streams whose individual scales and trajectories are not disclosed.

### 3. Revenue Streams That Remain Unquantified

**a) Absolute Starlink Mobile revenue:** Only growth deltas are disclosed (+$632M in FY2025; +$85M in Q1 2026). V1 Mobile satellites began deploying January 2024, so the FY2023 base was likely near zero, but the FY2024 base is unknown. The 7.4M monthly unique devices metric is disclosed, but without a corresponding revenue figure, mobile ARPU cannot be calculated from actuals (only market-sizing assumptions of $2–$18/month by income tier are provided).

**b) Absolute Starshield / government revenue:** Only growth deltas are disclosed (+$1,250M in FY2024; +$193M in FY2025; −$175M in Q1 2026). The FY2023 base is unknown, so absolute government revenue cannot be chained forward. The Q1 2026 decline of $175M highlights the lumpy, contract-driven nature of this stream.

**c) Absolute aviation, maritime, land mobility, and fixed-site enterprise revenue:** These are referenced qualitatively (industries served include "construction, agriculture, retail, telecom, hospitality, aviation, maritime, and land mobility") but never quantified individually. The Q1 2026 narrative groups them as "aviation, maritime, and other enterprise business" with a +$209M delta, but no absolute level or prior-period comparable.

**d) Consumer Kit vs. E&G Kit split:** Can only be bounded, not determined. For Q1 2026, the ARPU × subscriber framework (under linear growth) implies negative E&G kit, which is impossible—indicating the average subscriber count exceeded the linear-growth estimate. The framework only approximately reconciles for annual periods and breaks down for Q1 2026.

**e) Geographic revenue split:** No quantitative domestic vs. international or regional breakdown is disclosed. ARPU decline is attributed qualitatively to "international expansion and the addition of lower-priced service plans," but the geographic revenue mix is not quantified.

**f) Connectivity-specific deferred revenue and backlog:** Total deferred revenue ($12,116M at Dec 31, 2025; $13,236M at Mar 31, 2026) and total backlog ($27,621M) are disclosed only at the consolidated level. The filing states deferred revenue "primarily relates to Space agreements and Connectivity enterprise and government contracts," but no Connectivity-specific carve-out is provided.

**g) Subscriber-level economics:** Churn (gross or net), customer acquisition cost, lifetime value, net subscriber additions, ARPU by plan type (Residential vs. Roam vs. Business Priority), and ARPU by geography (actuals) are all undisclosed.

**h) Kit unit economics:** Kit shipment volumes, average selling price, kit gross margin, and terminal manufacturing cost are not disclosed.

**i) Enterprise/government customer metrics:** Number of enterprise customers, number of government customers, average contract value, and revenue retention rates are not disclosed. (The filing does note that since 2023, no Enterprise customer contributing >$750K annual revenue has voluntarily discontinued service.)

---

### 4. Modeling Limitations Following from This Disclosure Structure

**Limitation 1 — Cannot decompose E&G into absolute sub-component levels.**
The narrative provides only year-over-year growth deltas for mobile, government, and enterprise sub-components, without absolute bases. Because the FY2023 starting split between enterprise and government within the $1,052M E&G total is unknown, absolute revenue levels for Starlink Mobile, Starshield, aviation, maritime, and other enterprise streams cannot be derived. A modeler cannot build separate revenue trajectories for these streams.

**Limitation 2 — Inconsistent sub-categorization across periods prevents clean chaining.**
The E&G sub-component taxonomy shifts across periods:
- FY2024 narrative: two buckets (enterprise connectivity, government connectivity)
- FY2025 narrative: three buckets, with mobile nested within enterprise ("inclusive of")
- Q1 2026 narrative: three buckets, with mobile broken out parallel to "aviation, maritime, and other enterprise"

These shifts mean growth deltas cannot be cleanly chained across periods to reconstruct absolute levels. Additionally, the Q1 2026 sub-components ($209M + $85M − $175M = $119M) do not precisely reconcile to the disclosed total E&G growth of $126M, leaving a ~$7M unexplained residual.

**Limitation 3 — Cannot separate hardware (kit) from service economics by customer type.**
The products/services split is disclosed only at the company-wide level (all products = Connectivity). The consumer vs. E&G split bundles kit and service together. Without kit volumes, ASP, or margins, a modeler cannot assess the hardware subsidy strategy, kit contribution margin, or the service-vs-hardware revenue mix by customer segment.

**Limitation 4 — ARPU × subscriber framework only approximately reconciles and breaks down for quarterly periods.**
The ARPU metric is defined using *average* subscribers, but only period-end subscriber counts are disclosed. Under a linear-growth assumption, the framework approximately reconciles to the Consumer line for annual periods (implying ~$744M consumer kit in FY2025), but produces impossible results (negative E&G kit) for Q1 2026. This means quarterly consumer service vs. kit splits cannot be reliably inferred.

**Limitation 5 — Cannot model geographic mix shifts quantitatively.**
ARPU has declined 33% from $99 (FY2023) to $66 (Q1 2026), attributed to international expansion and lower-priced plans. Without a geographic revenue split, a modeler cannot decompose ARPU decline into volume mix (geography) vs. price mix (plan tier) effects, limiting the ability to project future ARPU trajectories or model geographic expansion economics.

**Limitation 6 — Cannot assess subscriber retention or lifetime economics.**
No churn, CAC, LTV, or net retention metrics are disclosed. The Service Line definition (one subscriber = one service line, not one end user) further complicates cohort analysis. Without these, sustainable growth rates and subscriber lifetime value cannot be modeled.

**Limitation 7 — Cannot isolate government (Starshield) contract lumpiness.**
Government revenue declined $175M in Q1 2026 after growing $1,250M in FY2024 and $193M in FY2025. Without absolute levels, contract timing details, or backlog allocation, the volatility and forward visibility of this stream cannot be modeled. The cost-to-cost input method for Starshield means revenue recognition depends on cost incurrence patterns that are not separately disclosed.

**Limitation 8 — Cannot model Starlink Mobile unit economics separately.**
Mobile revenue is embedded within E&G, with only growth deltas disclosed. The 7.4M monthly unique devices metric and ~30 MNO partnerships are disclosed, but without a corresponding revenue figure, mobile ARPU, revenue per device, or MNO contract economics cannot be derived from actuals.

**Limitation 9 — Deferred revenue and backlog cannot be allocated to Connectivity.**
This limits forward revenue visibility for the Connectivity segment specifically. Since deferred revenue "primarily relates to Space agreements and Connectivity enterprise and government contracts," a significant portion of the $13,236M deferred revenue balance is Connectivity-related, but the exact amount and its expected recognition timing are not separable.

**Limitation 10 — Segment gross margin is not directly disclosed.**
Only segment income from operations ($4,423M in FY2025) and segment Adjusted EBITDA ($7,168M in FY2025) are disclosed. Without a separate cost-of-revenue line for the segment, gross margin cannot be calculated, limiting analysis of hardware vs. service cost structures and operating leverage.

---

### Summary

The Connectivity segment's disclosure structure supports confident derivation of **total services vs. products revenue** and **Consumer vs. E&G revenue** at the top line. It also supports **approximate** inference of consumer service vs. kit revenue via the ARPU × subscriber framework (for annual periods). However, the absolute revenue levels of **Starlink Mobile, Starshield/government, aviation, maritime, and other enterprise sub-streams** remain unquantified—only growth deltas are provided, and these deltas use inconsistent categorizations across periods that prevent clean reconstruction. The resulting modeling limitations are significant: a modeler cannot build bottom-up sub-segment revenue models, cannot separate hardware from service economics by customer type, cannot model geographic mix shifts quantitatively, cannot assess subscriber retention/lifetime economics, and cannot allocate deferred revenue or backlog to Connectivity. These gaps are particularly material for the E&G line, which grew from $1,052M (FY2023) to $4,179M (FY2025)—a 4× increase driven by streams whose individual scales and trajectories are not disclosed.
